016package org.consensusj.jsonrpc;
017
018import java.io.IOException;
019
020/**
021 * JSON RPC Exception
022 * <p>
023 * The {@link JsonRpcErrorException} subclass includes a {@link JsonRpcError} object which can be used
024 * in server implementations to pass error information to the server's response serialization layer which
025 * can include the {@link JsonRpcError} in the {@link JsonRpcResponse} and in client implementations it can
026 * be used for a client to find the {@link JsonRpcError} that was returned.
027 * <p>
028 * The {@link JsonRpcStatusException} subclass contains support for HTTP response code and message.
029 * <p>
030 * TODO: Rethink the differences between the two subclasses in light of Bitcoin (and possibly other)
031 * implementations returning HTTP status codes along with JsonRpcError responses. Bitcoin even incorrectly
032 * returns a 500 for invalid parameters. I think I originally assumed that there would be either an
033 * HTTP status error or a JSON-RPC error, but in reality responses can probably have neither, either, or both.
034 * TODO: See Issue #352
035 * <p>
036 * Update: Bitcoin Core v28.0 added strict adherence to JSON-RPC 2.0, so we should probably move forward on
037 * the rethink/refactor and make sure that we handle JSON-RPC 2.0/Bitcoin Core v28+ very well with some level
038 * of support for earlier versions, too.
039 * @see <a href="https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/2960">Bitcoin Core Issue: Support JSON-RPC 2.0</a>
040 * @see <a href="https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/27101">Bitcoin Core PR: Support JSON-RPC 2.0 when requested by client</a>
041 */
042public class JsonRpcException extends IOException {
043
044    public JsonRpcException(String message) {
045        super(message);
046    }
047
048    public JsonRpcException(String message, Throwable cause) {
049        super(message, cause);
050    }
051
052}
